purpose: Maps dependencies between concepts and patterns so you know what to learn before tackling later material.

concept_chain.why: If you don't understand that LLMs predict the next word from context, you can't understand why pattern examples work, why CoT improves accuracy, or why ReAct uses a Thought-Action-Observation loop.
Understanding that LLM outputs vary between runs is foundational to understanding why prompt engineering techniques improve reliability but never guarantee determinism.
You need to understand that prompts can be rules, reminders, and conversation context — not just questions — before patterns like Persistent Instructions and Ask for Input make sense.
The intuition that LLMs continue patterns from training is the foundation for understanding why 'act as a doctor' works, why examples teach new tasks, and why templates produce consistent formatting.

needed_for: Game Play Pattern (M5V1) — game master is a persona
concept_chain.why: Audience Persona is the dual of Persona. Game Play uses a persona for the game master role. Understanding the basic 'act as X' mechanism is prerequisite.

needed_for: Alternative Approaches (refined questions often feed in)
concept_chain.why: Improving questions is a fundamental skill that recurs in more advanced patterns.

needed_for: Chain of Thought (M4V5) — same decomposition idea
ReAct (M4V6) — extends decomposition with tool use
concept_chain.why: CoT is essentially a simpler version of decomposition. ReAct is decomposition with tool use. Understanding Cognitive Verifier first makes CoT and ReAct click.

needed_for: Game Play Pattern (M5V1) — game master asks questions
Ask for Input Pattern (M6V1) — control handoff concept
concept_chain.why: Game Play uses flipped interaction to ask quiz questions. Ask for Input is essentially 'do a mini flipped interaction at the end of setup.'

needed_for: ReAct (M4V6) — uses few-shot for Thought/Action format
concept_chain.why: Few-shot with intermediate steps is the foundation for ReAct. Most advanced patterns benefit from few-shot examples.
ReAct is CoT + tool use. You need to understand CoT before ReAct makes sense.

needed_for: Outline Expansion (M6V3) — uses ReAct-like reasoning for hierarchical expansion
concept_chain.why: ReAct's tool-use loop is the conceptual ancestor of AI agents. Outline Expansion uses a similar reasoning pattern at a higher level.

needed_for: Menu Actions (M6V4) — uses template-like command definitions
concept_chain.why: Menu Actions defines trigger commands using a template-like format. Understanding placeholders is foundational.
Custom notation is an advanced output control technique used in sophisticated prompt systems.

needed_for: Outline Expansion (M6V3) — hierarchical filling of missing pieces
concept_chain.why: Outline Expansion is a hierarchical version of Recipe Pattern.

needed_for: Often combined with Ask for Input (M6V1)
concept_chain.why: Alternative Approaches is the canonical example of why you need Ask for Input — to wait for the user to specify the task.
Almost every pattern that sets up rules for future application benefits from Ask for Input to prevent premature generation.
Any prompt that runs across many turns needs Tail Generation to prevent rule-forgetting.
Real prompts combine 2-5 patterns. Understanding which patterns work well together (Persona + Ask for Input, Outline + Menu Actions, etc.) is the capstone skill.

learning_path_recommendation.rationale: M1-M2 build foundation (LLM mechanics, prompt basics). M3-M4 add core pattern skills (questions, few-shot, reasoning). M5-M6 add advanced workflows (templates, composition, maintenance).

critical_path_concepts: Next-word prediction (M1V4) — without this, nothing else makes sense
Pattern activation (M2V2) — foundation for all pattern work
Persona Pattern (M2V5) — most used pattern in the course
Chain of Thought (M4V5) — most reliable accuracy boost
